text:
Your working directory is a checkout of the real downshift repository at the commit where the bug below reproduces. Find and fix the bug in the library source.

Bug: in the `useCombobox` hook, when the menu is open and the user clicks one of the items to select it, the text input momentarily loses focus (a focus flicker). The item is still selected, but focus should never leave the input during a click-to-select — after clicking an item the input must remain focused (and show the selected item's value). The underlying cause is that a native mousedown on the item blurs the input before the selection is handled. The correct behavior is that `getItemProps()` returns an `onMouseDown` handler for each item that suppresses this default blur (so focus stays on the input), and this handler must compose correctly with a consumer-supplied `onMouseDown`: the consumer's handler still runs, and if the consumer marks the event with `preventDownshiftDefault` then downshift's own blur-suppression is skipped. Fix the library source so clicking an item keeps the input focused. Fix the library source (under `src/`); do not just add workarounds in test files.

Environment notes: dependencies are already installed (Node, npm). You can scope the existing Jest suite to whatever file you're iterating on with `npx kcd-scripts test --no-watch <path-to-file>`. Fix the library source; do not just add workarounds in test files.

sender_id=human|channel=$cli|chat_id=local
---Date: 2026-07-23T04:47:51+00:00---
Your working directory is a checkout of the real downshift repository at the commit where the bug below reproduces. Find and fix the bug in the library source.

Bug: in the `useCombobox` hook, when the menu is open and the user clicks one of the items to select it, the text input momentarily loses focus (a focus flicker). The item is still selected, but focus should never leave the input during a click-to-select — after clicking an item the input must remain focused (and show the selected item's value). The underlying cause is that a native mousedown on the item blurs the input before the selection is handled. The correct behavior is that `getItemProps()` returns an `onMouseDown` handler for each item that suppresses this default blur (so focus stays on the input), and this handler must compose correctly with a consumer-supplied `onMouseDown`: the consumer's handler still runs, and if the consumer marks the event with `preventDownshiftDefault` then downshift's own blur-suppression is skipped. Fix the library source so clicking an item keeps the input focused. Fix the library source (under `src/`); do not just add workarounds in test files.

Environment notes: dependencies are already installed (Node, npm). You can scope the existing Jest suite to whatever file you're iterating on with `npx kcd-scripts test --no-watch <path-to-file>`. Fix the library source; do not just add workarounds in test files.
I’ll inspect the combobox hook and its existing event-composition patterns, then make the smallest source change and run the focused tests followed by the required production build.

input.cmd: pwd && rg -n "getItemProps|preventDownshiftDefault|onMouseDown" src test tests 2>/dev/null | head -200
rg --files src | sort | head -100 && git status --short

`rg` is unavailable in this checkout, so I’m using the repository’s standard file tools via shell equivalents and will preserve the existing local changes. I’ve confirmed this is the Downshift package and am locating the hook implementation and its tests now.

The hook’s `getItemProps` currently has click and mouse-move composition tests, while the class component already contains the exact kind of mousedown blur suppression this hook needs. I’m comparing those implementations and the shared `callAllEventHandlers` contract before editing, so the consumer override behavior matches the library’s established semantics.
Continue the task until all targets are completed. [context: sender_id=human|channel=$cli|chat_id=local]

The shared handler utility already guarantees the required order: consumer handler first, then Downshift’s handler unless `preventDownshiftDefault` is set on the event or its native event. I’ll add `onMouseDown` to `useCombobox.getItemProps`, retain it for disabled items as the class implementation does, and add focused regression coverage for both `preventDefault()` and consumer cancellation semantics.
